title.en: S3 Deep Dive: Storage Classes & Lifecycle Rules

description.en: Understand S3's storage classes, lifecycle transitions, and durability model — and build the cost calculator that justifies every archival policy you will ever write.

story_variants.en:
## Eleven Nines and a Growing Bill

S3 is the closest thing the cloud has to bedrock: designed for **99.999999999% (eleven nines) durability**, storing objects redundantly across a minimum of three Availability Zones for standard classes, scaling to exabytes without you pre-provisioning anything. Enterprises treat it as the default landing place for everything — logs, data lakes, backups, ML training sets, compliance archives. Which is precisely why S3 quietly becomes a top-three line item on the bill: data flows in forever and almost never flows out. The discipline that fixes this is storage-class management, and it is one of the highest ROI, lowest risk optimizations in all of AWS.

## The Storage Class Ladder

Every S3 object lives in exactly one storage class. Same API, same bucket, radically different economics:

| Class | Availability design | Retrieval | Best for |
|---|---|---|---|
| S3 Standard | Multi-AZ | Immediate | Hot, frequently accessed data |
| S3 Intelligent-Tiering | Multi-AZ | Immediate | Unknown or shifting access patterns |
| S3 Standard-IA | Multi-AZ | Immediate, per-GB retrieval fee | Monthly-touched data, backups |
| S3 One Zone-IA | **Single AZ** | Immediate, retrieval fee | Recreatable secondary copies |
| S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval | Multi-AZ | Milliseconds, higher retrieval fee | Archives needing instant access |
| S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval | Multi-AZ | Minutes to hours | Rarely restored archives |
| S3 Glacier Deep Archive | Multi-AZ | Up to ~12 hours (standard) | 7-10 year compliance retention |

The pricing **model** is the key insight, not any specific number: storage cost per GB-month falls dramatically as you move down the ladder, while retrieval cost and latency rise. Colder classes also carry **minimum storage durations** (30 days for the IA classes, 90 for Glacier Instant/Flexible, 180 for Deep Archive) and **minimum billable object sizes** for some classes (IA classes bill small objects as 128 KB). Delete or transition early and you pay the remainder anyway.

> **Pitfall:** Lifecycle-transitioning millions of tiny objects to Glacier can *increase* cost: each transition is a billed request, each archived object carries metadata overhead, and 128 KB minimums bite. Aggregate small objects (tar/parquet) before archiving.

## Intelligent-Tiering: The 'I Don't Know' Answer

When access patterns are genuinely unknown, **S3 Intelligent-Tiering** monitors each object and moves it automatically between frequent-access, infrequent-access (after 30 days untouched), and archive-instant tiers (after 90) — with optional deep archive tiers — for a small monthly monitoring charge per object and **no retrieval fees** between the automatic tiers. For data lakes with unpredictable query patterns, it is the Well-Architected default. For data with a *known* schedule (logs that are hot for a week, then dead), explicit lifecycle rules are cheaper because you skip the monitoring charge.

## Lifecycle Rules: Policy as Janitor

A lifecycle configuration attaches rules to a bucket (optionally filtered by prefix, tags, or object size) with two action types:

- **Transition actions** — move objects between classes after N days from creation.
- **Expiration actions** — delete objects (or permanently remove noncurrent versions, or abort incomplete multipart uploads) after N days.

A canonical enterprise log-bucket policy:

- Day 0-30: S3 Standard (dashboards and on-call queries are hot)
- Day 30: transition to Standard-IA (occasional investigation access)
- Day 90: transition to Glacier Instant Retrieval or Flexible Retrieval
- Day 365: transition to Deep Archive (compliance only)
- Day 2555 (7 years): expire

Always include the unglamorous rule everyone forgets: **AbortIncompleteMultipartUpload after 7 days**. Failed multipart uploads invisibly accumulate billable parts that appear in no object listing.

> **War story:** A fintech discovered 40+ TB of orphaned multipart parts from a flaky uploader that had been retrying for two years. One lifecycle rule, one line of config, immediate permanent savings. Check `aws s3api list-multipart-uploads` on your oldest buckets — nearly every mature account has some.

## Versioning, Replication, and the Safety Toolkit

Storage classes interact with the rest of S3's enterprise features:

- **Versioning** keeps noncurrent versions — which also cost money. Lifecycle rules can transition and expire noncurrent versions separately (`NoncurrentVersionTransition` / `NoncurrentVersionExpiration`).
- **Object Lock** (governance or compliance mode) provides WORM retention for regulatory data — compliance mode cannot be shortened by anyone, including root.
- **Replication** (same-region or cross-region) copies objects to another bucket, optionally another account — the backbone of DR and log-archive patterns. Replication can write directly into a colder class at the destination.
- **Block Public Access** stays on at the account level in every enterprise; public data goes through CloudFront, not public buckets.

## Decision Framework: Classing New Data

1. **Known-hot forever?** Standard. Do not overthink.
2. **Known cooling schedule?** Explicit lifecycle transitions timed to the access curve.
3. **Unknown pattern, objects > 128 KB?** Intelligent-Tiering.
4. **Recreatable derivative data?** One Zone-IA is acceptable; nothing else belongs in a single AZ.
5. **Compliance archive, touched never?** Straight to Deep Archive — but batch small objects first.
6. **Before archiving anything, price the restore path.** Retrieval fees and hours-long restore times are fine until an auditor wants 5 TB by Friday.

Run S3 Storage Lens (the free organization-wide dashboards) quarterly and let **Storage Class Analysis** observe real access patterns before you write transition days into policy. The cheapest byte is the one you expire; the most expensive is the one you archived wrong and restored in a panic. Lifecycle policy is where storage engineering meets fiduciary duty — write it like it will be audited, because it will.

description.en: The storage class ladder: per-GB cost falls as you descend, retrieval cost and latency rise. Lifecycle rules automate the descent.

left.label: Everything in S3 Standard forever, reviewed never

right.label: Lifecycle policy timed to the real access curve, plus expiration

description.en: Data has a temperature curve. Matching class to temperature is a config file, not a project — and it compounds monthly.

explanation.en: Rules target prefixes or tags — one bucket can carry different policies for different data.
After 30 days, objects move to Standard-IA. Note the 30-day minimum storage duration in IA — transitioning earlier still bills the minimum.
Expiration is the most powerful cost lever of all: deleted data costs nothing. Seven years here (2555 days) matches common compliance retention.

lines: $ echo 'orphaned multipart uploads found — add AbortIncompleteMultipartUpload rule'

description.en: The classic hidden cost: incomplete multipart uploads invisible to object listings, cleaned by one lifecycle rule.
The lifecycle of a log object across seven years — each stage cheaper per GB than the last.

instruction.en: Build an S3 lifecycle cost calculator using FICTIONAL per-GB-month rate units (defined in RATES — these are teaching units, not real AWS prices; real pricing varies by region and time). For a dataset of DATA_GB gigabytes, write monthly_cost(gb, storage_class) returning gb * RATES[storage_class]. Print one line per class, in the order STANDARD, STANDARD_IA, GLACIER_IR, DEEP_ARCHIVE, formatted exactly as '<CLASS>: <cost> units/month' (cost printed as a plain float, e.g. 11500.0). Then compute the percentage saved in month 13 — when lifecycle rules have moved the data to DEEP_ARCHIVE versus leaving it in STANDARD — as round((standard - deep) / standard * 100, 1) and print 'Lifecycle saves <pct>% in month 13 vs Standard'. This is the exact justification slide every archival policy proposal needs.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
RATES = {
    "STANDARD": 23.0,
    "STANDARD_IA": 12.5,
    "GLACIER_IR": 4.0,
    "DEEP_ARCHIVE": 1.0,
}
DATA_GB = 500

def monthly_cost(gb, storage_class):
    # TODO: return gb * the class rate
    pass

# TODO: print one line per class in RATES order:
#   f"{cls}: {cost} units/month"
# TODO: pct = round((standard_cost - deep_cost) / standard_cost * 100, 1)
# TODO: print f"Lifecycle saves {pct}% in month 13 vs Standard"
practice_challenges.expected_output:
STANDARD: 11500.0 units/month
STANDARD_IA: 6250.0 units/month
GLACIER_IR: 2000.0 units/month
DEEP_ARCHIVE: 500.0 units/month
Lifecycle saves 95.7% in month 13 vs Standard

en: monthly_cost is one line: return gb * RATES[storage_class]. 500 * 23.0 = 11500.0 prints as '11500.0' with a plain f-string.
Iterate the RATES dict directly — Python 3.7+ preserves insertion order, which matches the required output order.

practice_challenges.test_code:
assert monthly_cost(500, "STANDARD") == 11500.0
assert monthly_cost(500, "STANDARD_IA") == 6250.0
assert monthly_cost(500, "GLACIER_IR") == 2000.0
assert monthly_cost(500, "DEEP_ARCHIVE") == 500.0
assert monthly_cost(0, "STANDARD") == 0.0

q.en: Which storage class stores data in only ONE Availability Zone?
You transition an object to S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval and delete it after 20 days. What happens to billing?

en: You are charged for the 90-day minimum storage duration
Deletion from Glacier is free but retrieval is not
The object cannot be deleted before 90 days

q.en: A data lake has completely unpredictable access patterns and mostly multi-MB objects. Which class does AWS guidance point to?
Which lifecycle rule reclaims storage that never appears in any object listing?
